verses.text: Weary of life, Job resolves to pour out his complaint and asks God not to condemn him without showing why: 'is it good unto thee that thou shouldest oppress?' He pleads to understand his affliction. The believer may bring even his bewildered questions honestly to God.

Weary of life, Job resolves to pour out his complaint and asks God not to condemn him without showing why: 'is it good unto thee that thou shouldest oppress?' He pleads to understand his affliction. The believer may bring even his bewildered questions honestly to God.

Weary of life, Job resolves to pour out his complaint and asks God not to condemn him without showing why: 'is it good unto thee that thou shouldest oppress?' He pleads to understand his affliction. The believer may bring even his bewildered questions honestly to God.

He asks whether God has eyes of flesh, seeing as man sees, when He knows Job is not wicked — yet none can deliver from His hand. He appeals to God's perfect knowledge. The God who searches us also knows the truth of us better than our accusers do.

He asks whether God has eyes of flesh, seeing as man sees, when He knows Job is not wicked — yet none can deliver from His hand. He appeals to God's perfect knowledge. The God who searches us also knows the truth of us better than our accusers do.

Job remembers that God's own hands made and fashioned him, granting him life and favour, His care preserving his spirit. He recalls the mercy of his making. The God who formed us with such tenderness will not finally cast away the work of His hands.

Job remembers that God's own hands made and fashioned him, granting him life and favour, His care preserving his spirit. He recalls the mercy of his making. The God who formed us with such tenderness will not finally cast away the work of His hands.

Yet now, Job feels, God watches his every step: if he sins, woe; if righteous, he dare not lift his head, filled with confusion. He feels trapped on every side. In such darkness the soul imagines God only as a watchful judge, forgetting He is also a Father.

Yet now, Job feels, God watches his every step: if he sins, woe; if righteous, he dare not lift his head, filled with confusion. He feels trapped on every side. In such darkness the soul imagines God only as a watchful judge, forgetting He is also a Father.

Why, then, Job asks, was he ever brought from the womb, wishing he had been carried straight from birth to the grave. Despair revisits its old refrain. Pain still tempts him to count his very life a mistake, though God's purpose in it stands.

Why, then, Job asks, was he ever brought from the womb, wishing he had been carried straight from birth to the grave. Despair revisits its old refrain. Pain still tempts him to count his very life a mistake, though God's purpose in it stands.

He begs God to let him alone for his few remaining days, that he might find a little comfort before going to 'the land of darkness.' He pleads only for a moment's relief. The sufferer's faint cry is heard by God, who will yet bring him into light.
